preFix.title: Woven rya knot wall hanging
preFix.subtitle: A shaggy, textured wall hanging made with rya knots tied on a plain-weave ground, approximately 40 × 60 cm with pile
preFix.excerpt: Rya is a Scandinavian technique in which knotted pile is tied onto a plain-weave warp at intervals, producing a long, shaggy surface over a woven cloth ground. The finished hanging is richly textured and holds colour particularly well in blended or variegated yarns.
preFix.sourceNotes: Rya knotting on a woven ground is a Scandinavian textile tradition with documented history in Finland and Scandinavia from the medieval period. Technique synthesised from general rya weaving practice.

content.text: A rya wall hanging is built from two alternating elements: rows of
rya knots
that project from the surface as loose
pile
, and rows of plain
ground-weave
that lock the knots in place. The
warp

is set at a relatively open sett (4-5 ends per cm) to make the knot-tying easy and to allow the pile yarn to fill out. Blended and variegated yarns work exceptionally well for rya pile: the long cut ends mix visually in a way that short weft passes in tapestry cannot replicate.

items.name: Warp yarn, smooth cotton or linen, DK weight
Pile yarn, wool, bulky or aran weight, in 2-4 colours
Ground weft yarn, matching or neutral
Rigid heddle loom or frame loom, 45 cm weaving width

content.text: Method
Step 1: Set up the warp

Wind a cotton or linen warp at 4-5 ends per cm for a 40 cm wide hanging. A smooth, strong warp yarn is important: the knots are tied tightly around each pair of warp ends and a weak warp will break during knotting. Thread through the rigid heddle and wind onto the back beam. The warp length should be the planned hanging length plus 40 cm for loom waste at each end.
Step 2: Cut the pile yarn

Decide on the pile length: 10-15 cm cut lengths give a generous, shaggy pile. Cut many lengths in each colour before beginning: one row of knots across a 40 cm width uses approximately 60-80 pieces. Wrap the pile yarn around a piece of card at the desired cut length and slice along one edge to produce uniform lengths quickly. Prepare enough for the whole hanging before sitting at the loom.
Step 3: Tie the rya knots

Working from left to right along the warp, take one cut pile length and hold it horizontally behind two adjacent warp ends. Bring the left tail up and over the left warp end, then down between the two ends. Bring the right tail up and over the right warp end, then down between the two ends. Both tails should now hang down in front of the warp between the pair. Pull both tails firmly to seat the knot against the ground weave. Continue across the full warp width, varying the colour arrangement as planned.
Step 4: Weave the ground rows

After each row of knots, weave 2-4 plain picks of the ground weft to lock the knots in place. Beat firmly after each pick. The ground picks compress the knots and prevent them from sliding along the warp. The pile from the previous row will project forward through the shed as you weave the ground picks: this is normal. Work down the hanging, alternating knot rows with ground rows. The pile obscures the ground weft entirely in the finished piece.
Step 5: Cut from the loom and finish

Cut the hanging from the loom leaving 5-6 cm of warp at the top. Loop the top warp ends over the wooden dowel and tie in pairs with an overhand knot to secure. Trim the pile to an even length if desired, or leave it slightly uneven for a more organic texture. Steam the pile lightly with a clothes steamer held 10 cm away to open the fibres.

items.fix: Weave at least 4 plain picks between each knot row. Beat firmly after each pick to compress the ground and pin the knots.
items.cause: Too few ground picks were woven between knot rows to lock the knots in place
items.problem: The knots slide up and down the warp after tying

items.fix: Keep the knot tails pointing downward rather than pulling outward. Check the width at each row and ease the selvedge ends back to the correct position before weaving the next ground pick.
items.cause: The knot tails are being pulled outward during tying, drawing the selvedge warp ends inward
items.problem: The hanging is narrower at the knot rows than at the ground rows

items.fix: At 4-5 ends per cm, pile yarn of aran weight or heavier produces the best texture. Shorter cuts (under 10 cm) lie flatter. Increase pile length to 12-15 cm and use a heavier yarn.
items.cause: The pile yarn is too fine or was cut too short for the sett
items.problem: The pile looks flat rather than full and shaggy
